statement.markdown: There is a chessboard of size n x n. Determine how many ways you can place the queen on the board so that it does not attack each other. Write a backtracking algorithm that correctly counts the number of ways for placing queens without attacking other queens. However, there is a logical bug in the code which prevents it from counting all the possible ways.
statement.inputFormat: Take a positive integer n as input representing the size of the board.
statement.outputFormat: Return the number of ways to place n queens on the board such that no two queens attack each other.
statement.notes: The code uses backtracking but contains a logical error that prevents it from counting all possible placements.

examples.explanation: There are 2 possible placements of 4 queens on a 4x4 board. The first placement is when there are no queens in the first row, and the second when there is a queen in the first column.

hints.text: Understand how backtracking works and how to use it for placing queens on a board. Identify the logical error in the buggy code.

editorial.title: N-Queens III: Backtracking Bug Fix Editorial
editorial.intuition: To find the number of ways of placing queens on the board, we can use a backtracking algorithm that starts placing a queen in each column from left to right. If it is possible to place a queen in the current column without being attacked by any of the queens already placed, we continue to the next column. If we can place all the queens, then we get a valid solution.
editorial.bruteForce: We can solve the problem by brute force and generate all the permutations of placing queens on the board and check if they are valid. However, this approach is not efficient for large inputs.
editorial.optimalSolution: To solve the problem efficiently, we use a backtracking algorithm that tracks the row and column of each queen. We start placing a queen in each column from left to right, and we use a check function to see if it is possible to place a queen in the current column without being attacked by any of the queens already placed.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The backtracking approach correctly counts all the possible ways of placing queens on the board without attacking each other.
editorial.dryRun: The backtracking approach works as follows: 1. Start by placing the first queen. 2. Check if it is possible to place the queen without being attacked by any of the queens already placed. If not, backtrack. 3. If it is possible to place the queen, continue to the next column. 4. Repeat the process until all queens are placed or it is not possible to place the queen.
